1137	\begin{lemma}
1138	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); Lean declaration removed in iter-218 after the associator re-route onto `tensorobj_restrict_iso`.
1139	
1140	\leanok
1141	[Local injectivity of a whiskered localizer morphism (stalkwise, flatness-free)]
1142	  \label{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1143	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1144	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
1145	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the sheafification localizer (the locally
1146	  bijective morphisms). Concretely the site carries the
1147	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) property for \(\mathtt{Ab}\), so \(J.W\) is
1148	  exactly the class of \(J\)-locally bijective morphisms; this is recorded as a
1149	  typeclass hypothesis \([J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}]\) on
1150	  the statement. Let \(F\) be an \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of
1151	  \(R\)-modules, and let \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of
1152	  \(R\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
1153	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R).\mathrm{map}\,g\) lies in \(J.W\). Then the
1154	  left-whiskered morphism
1155	  \[
1156	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
1157	  \]
1158	  is \emph{\(J\)-locally injective}. No flatness and no local-triviality
1159	  hypothesis on \(F\) is required.
1160	
1161	  This is the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\)-field content (the injectivity half) of the
1162	  instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}), the load-bearing
1163	  field of the \emph{full-generality} route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path
1164	  (arbitrary \(F\)). For the relative Picard group law, however, the whisker object
1165	  \(F\) supplied by the sole consumer --- the locally-trivial-scoped associator
1166	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- is always \emph{locally trivial}
1167	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), and in that case local injectivity
1168	  of \(F \triangleleft g\) admits a markedly simpler, stalk-free proof, given as the
1169	  PRIMARY route below. The general stalkwise argument (ingredients (d.1)/(d.2)) is
1170	  retained only as the FALLBACK, required if the full route~(e) monoidal category on
1171	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) is later wanted; the locally-trivial
1172	  primary route already suffices for the group law.
1173	\end{lemma}
1174	
1175	\begin{proof}
1176	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map,
1177	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1178	
1179	  \emph{PRIMARY route (\(F\) locally trivial --- the consumer's case; sheaf-level,
1180	  no stalks, no (d.2)).} Suppose \(F\) is locally trivial
1181	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), which is exactly the hypothesis its
1182	  sole consumer --- the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- supplies.
1183	  \(J\)-local injectivity of a morphism is \emph{local on a covering family}: it
1184	  suffices to produce a cover of \(X\) on each member of which \(F \triangleleft g\)
1185	  restricts to a locally injective morphism. Take a trivialising open cover
1186	  \(\{V\}\) of \(X\) for \(F\), so that \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) on each \(V\).
1187	  On such a \(V\):
1188	  \begin{itemize}
1189	    \item the substrate--restriction compatibility \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1190	      gives a canonical isomorphism
1191	      \[
1192	        (F \triangleleft g)\big|_V \;\cong\; (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)
1193	      \]
1194	      --- restriction along the open immersion \(V \hookrightarrow X\) commutes with
1195	      \(\otimes\), and the whiskering \(F \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g\)
1196	      is \(\otimes\) with an identity, so it restricts to \(\mathrm{id}_{F|_V}
1197	      \otimes (g|_V) = (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)\);
1198	    \item the trivialisation \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) together with the left
1199	      unitor \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_V \otimes_V (-) \cong
1200	      (-)\)) identifies \((F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V) \cong g|_V\).
1201	  \end{itemize}
1202	  Composing, \((F \triangleleft g)|_V\) is isomorphic to \(g|_V\). Now \(g \in J.W\),
1203	  and membership in \(J.W\) (local bijectivity) is stable under restriction along an
1204	  open immersion, so \(g|_V \in J.W\); in particular \(g|_V\) is \(J\)-locally
1205	  injective. An isomorphism preserves local injectivity, hence \((F \triangleleft
1206	  g)|_V\) is locally injective on \(V\). As the \(\{V\}\) cover \(X\) and local
1207	  injectivity is local on the cover, \(F \triangleleft g\) is \(J\)-locally
1208	  injective. \emph{No stalks and no (d.2) stalk--tensor commutation enter}; the
1209	  single comparison lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (a presheaf-level
1210	  comparison, decomposed into its two open-immersion base-change halves) carries the
1211	  argument, and thereby moves \emph{onto} the critical path.
1212	
1213	  This is \emph{not} the section-level route that tensors a bare section map \(g(V)\)
1214	  --- only injective --- and stalls on a \(\mathrm{Tor}_1\)/flatness obligation. Here
1215	  the argument is sheaf-level and uses that \(g\) is locally \emph{bijective} (a
1216	  \(J.W\) morphism), reduced through the unitor to \(g|_V\) (again a \(J.W\)
1217	  morphism); a mere injection is never tensored, so no flatness is needed.
1218	
1219	  \emph{FALLBACK route (arbitrary \(F\); stalkwise, required only for the
1220	  full-generality route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path).} For an
1221	  \emph{arbitrary} (not necessarily locally trivial) \(F\), local injectivity is
1222	  proved stalkwise, by the argument Mathlib uses for fixed-base presheaves in
1223	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\)
1224	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}), ported to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). A
1225	  morphism in \(J.W\) is, on the topological site of \(X\), a \emph{stalkwise
1226	  isomorphism}: at each point \(x\) the induced map on stalks \(g_x\) is an
1227	  isomorphism. The presheaf-of-modules tensor is sectionwise, so it commutes with
1228	  the stalk (a filtered colimit), giving
1229	  \[
1230	    (F \triangleleft g)_x
1231	      \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes_{R_x} g_x
1232	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \;\longrightarrow\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
1233	  \]
1234	  Tensoring an isomorphism \(g_x\) by the identity \(\mathrm{id}_{F_x}\) yields an
1235	  isomorphism --- no flatness is needed, since \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely
1236	  injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, in particular
1237	  locally bijective, in particular locally injective, for \emph{any} \(F\).
1238	
1239	  The stalkwise computation rests on three ingredients with distinct status at the
1240	  pinned commit:
1241	  \begin{enumerate}
1242	    \item[(d.1-done)] the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear stalk-map packaging
1243	      (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) and its companions, \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}):
1244	      a morphism of presheaves of modules induces an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear
1245	      map on stalks, characterised on germs, that becomes a linear equivalence once
1246	      the underlying \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map is an isomorphism. This is built
1247	      project-side, axiom-clean, and furnishes the \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step
1248	      above.
1249	    \item[(d.1-bridge)] (remaining) the site-\(J.W\) \(\Longleftrightarrow\)
1250	      stalkwise-iso characterisation on \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\): that a morphism lies
1251	      in \(J.W\) iff its stalk maps are isomorphisms at every point. The concrete
1252	      Mathlib assembly route uses the \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) structure
1253	      together with the topological stalk criteria --- local injectivity
1254	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) injectivity on stalks and local surjectivity
1255	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) surjectivity on stalks --- bridging the site-level
1256	      \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsLocallyInjective}\) / \(\mathtt{IsLocallySurjective}\)
1257	      to the \(\mathtt{TopCat.Presheaf}\) stalk versions. This half is still
1258	      Mathlib-absent at the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
1259	    \item[(d.2)] (remaining) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
1260	      \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk (a filtered colimit) with the relative
1261	      module-presheaf tensor, under which \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) is identified
1262	      with \(\mathrm{lTensor}\,F_x\,(g_x) = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) (using
1263	      that \(\mathtt{tensorLeft}\) / \(\mathtt{tensorRight}\) preserve filtered
1264	      colimits over a module category). This is genuinely Mathlib-absent over a
1265	      \emph{varying} ring and is the largest remaining piece.
1266	  \end{enumerate}
1267	  Mathlib supplies the analogues for presheaves valued in a fixed monoidal concrete
1268	  category, but not for modules over a varying ring; porting (d.1-bridge) and (d.2)
1269	  to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) is the remaining work. Once (d.2) lands, the
1270	  conclusion is immediate and flatness-free: by
1271	  \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\) the stalk map \(g_x\) is a
1272	  linear equivalence, and the linear-equivalence \(\mathrm{lTensor}\) by \(F_x\)
1273	  carries it to an isomorphism, finishing the proof.
1274	
1275	  This stalkwise fallback is required \emph{only} if the full-generality
1276	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) route~(e) --- the
1277	  monoidal category on \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), for an
1278	  arbitrary whisker object \(F\) --- is later wanted. For the relative Picard group
1279	  law the whisker objects are locally trivial, and the PRIMARY route above suffices,
1280	  trading the Mathlib-absent (d.2) for the single comparison
1281	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1282	\end{proof}
1283	
1284	\emph{Superseded route --- off path, not to be formalized.} The following lemma
1285	supplies the two whiskering-stability fields of the abandoned
1286	\((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}). The group law
1287	needs no such instance: the associator is built directly by gluing canonical local
1288	isomorphisms (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).
1289	This block is retained for the historical record only and must \emph{not} be
1290	formalized; its Lean declarations are being removed.

1292	\begin{lemma}
1293	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); Lean declaration removed in iter-218 after the associator re-route onto `tensorobj_restrict_iso`.
1294	[Flatness-free whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
1295	  \label{lem:whisker_of_W}
1296	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1297	  With notation as in \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: for an
1298	  \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of \(R\)-modules \(F\) and a morphism \(g\) whose
1299	  underlying additive-presheaf map lies in \(J.W\), both whiskerings again lie in
1300	  \(J.W\):
1301	  \[
1302	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; F \triangleleft g \in J.W,
1303	    \qquad
1304	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; g \triangleright F \in J.W .
1305	  \]
1306	  These two statements are precisely the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) and
1307	  \(\mathtt{whiskerRight}\) data required by the class
1308	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) for
1309	  the morphism property \(J.W\); together they furnish the instance
1310	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) of \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}. No flatness and no
1311	  local triviality is used --- they are the flatness-free replacements for the
1312	  flat variants of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}.
1313	\end{lemma}
1314	
1315	\begin{proof}
1316	  \uses{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1317	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of \(J.W\), it suffices to show
1318	  \(F \triangleleft g\) is both locally injective and locally surjective.
1319	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is free and needs no hypothesis on \(F\): the presheaf
1320	  tensor is right exact, so \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the locally-zero cokernel
1321	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\), which is therefore locally
1322	  zero (Mathlib \(\mathtt{isLocallySurjective\_whiskerLeft}\)). \emph{Local
1323	  injectivity} is \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. The two together
1324	  give \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
1325	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
1326	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf monoidal structure, which is an isomorphism
1327	  and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\).
1328	\end{proof}
1329	
1330	\emph{Superseded route --- off path, not to be formalized.} The following lemma is
1331	the route~(e) target: a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on all of
1332	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) from \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\). It is not
1333	needed. The group law consumes only the existence of the associator, unitor and
1334	braiding isomorphisms (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, assembled by hand
1335	rather than off a coherent monoidal category's \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)), and the
1336	associator is now built directly by
1337	gluing (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}). This
1338	block is retained for the historical record only and must \emph{not} be formalized;
1339	no Lean declaration realizes it and none should.

1341	\begin{lemma}
1342	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); Lean declaration removed in iter-218 after the associator re-route onto `tensorobj_restrict_iso`.
1343	[The \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal substrate (route~(e) target)]
1344	  \label{lem:jw_ismonoidal}
1345	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:whisker_of_W}
1346	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The sheafification localizer \(J.W\) on
1347	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{monoidal}: it carries an
1348	  instance
1349	  \[
1350	    J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal},
1351	  \]
1352	  whose two fields are the whiskering-stability lemmas \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}
1353	  (and which is multiplicative because sheafification inverts identities and
1354	  composites). Consequently, by Mathlib's
1355	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)
1356	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) applied to the already-monoidal category
1357	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\), the localization functor
1358	  \(L = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\) (which is the localization at
1359	  \(J.W\)), and the unit isomorphism \(L(\mathbf{1}) \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), the
1360	  category
1361	  \[
1362	    \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
1363	      \;=\; \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
1364	  \]
1365	  acquires a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure whose tensor is the substrate
1366	  \(\otimes_X\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}) and whose associator,
1367	  left/right unitors, braiding and \emph{all} coherence (pentagon, triangle,
1368	  naturalities) are derived by the API. This is the route~(e) target: the
1369	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors
1370	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso} are
1371	  then components of this derived monoidal structure rather than hand-assembled
1372	  composites.
1373	
1374	  This block states the route~(e) construction; it is currently
1375	  \emph{stated-but-unformalized} (no Lean declaration realizes the
1376	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance yet), pending the sole open obligation
1377	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. It mirrors Mathlib's
1378	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) construction one categorical level up.
1379	\end{lemma}
1380	
1381	\begin{proof}
1382	  \uses{lem:whisker_of_W}
1383	  Multiplicativity of \(J.W\) (closure under identities and composition) is part
1384	  of its being a localizer (\(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). The two
1385	  whiskering-stability fields of \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) are
1386	  exactly the left- and right-whiskering statements of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
1387	  Hence \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) holds. Sheafification of presheaves of
1388	  modules is a localization at \(J.W\) (the underlying identification of
1389	  sheafification with localization at the locally-bijective class), and the unit
1390	  presheaf sheafifies to \(\mathcal{O}_X\); feeding these to
1391	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) produces the \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
1392	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with all coherence derived, as in
1393	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}. No \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure enters
1394	  at any point.
1395	\end{proof}

1397	\begin{lemma}
1398	
1399	\leanok
1400	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on locally trivial objects]
1401	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
1402	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso}
1403	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1404	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
1405	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). There is an isomorphism
1406	  \[
1407	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
1408	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1409	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
1410	  \]
1411	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), natural in \(M, N, P\). The group law on
1412	  \(\otimes\)-iso-classes (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) consumes only
1413	  the \emph{existence} of this isomorphism, the proposition
1414	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \cong M \otimes_X (N
1415	  \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality coherence of it is
1416	  ever required.
1417	
1418	  The current Lean pin additionally carries \emph{locally trivial}
1419	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\), \cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial})
1420	  hypotheses on \(M, N, P\). The construction below produces the isomorphism for
1421	  arbitrary \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules; the hypotheses are retained only to match
1422	  the carrier the group law multiplies on, and are not used in the gluing.
1423	\end{lemma}
1424	
1425	\begin{proof}
1426	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1427	  The isomorphism is built by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through the
1428	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, with no
1429	  recourse to any whiskering or stalk apparatus. Fix a
1430	  common open cover \(\{U\}\) of \(X\). On each member \(U\) form the canonical
1431	  composite
1432	  \[
1433	    \bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\bigr)\big|_U
1434	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1435	    \bigl(M|_U \otimes_U N|_U\bigr) \otimes_U P|_U
1436	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1437	    M|_U \otimes_U \bigl(N|_U \otimes_U P|_U\bigr)
1438	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1439	    \bigl(M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\big|_U ,
1440	  \]
1441	  in which:
1442	  \begin{itemize}
1443	    \item the first arrow is two applications of the restriction-compatibility
1444	      isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} along the open immersion
1445	      \(U \hookrightarrow X\): first \((M \otimes_X N)|_U \cong M|_U \otimes_U
1446	      N|_U\) tensored with \(P|_U\), then \(\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X
1447	      P\bigr)|_U \cong (M \otimes_X N)|_U \otimes_U P|_U\);
1448	    \item the middle arrow is the \emph{canonical presheaf-level associator}
1449	      \(\alpha\) of the (symmetric) monoidal category
1450	      \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\)
1451	      (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\),
1452	      \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)), read on the restricted modules;
1453	    \item the third arrow is again two applications of
1454	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, in the opposite direction.
1455	  \end{itemize}
1456	  Each of the three arrows is a \emph{canonical} isomorphism: the
1457	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is induced
1458	  by the open immersion with no choices, hence natural in its module arguments, and
1459	  the presheaf associator \(\alpha\) is a fixed natural transformation. Therefore,
1460	  on a double overlap \(U \cap U'\), restricting the \(U\)-composite and the
1461	  \(U'\)-composite to \(U \cap U'\) yields the same morphism: each of the three
1462	  arrows commutes with the further restriction to \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1463	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the presheaf associator, so the two local
1464	  composites agree on the overlap.
1465	
1466	  Because the internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(-,-)\) of sheaves of
1467	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is itself a sheaf on \(X\), a family of morphisms defined
1468	  on the members of an open cover and agreeing on the overlaps glues to a single
1469	  global morphism. Applying this both to the local composites above and to their
1470	  local inverses produces a global morphism
1471	  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \to M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\) whose restriction
1472	  to each \(U\) is the local composite, together with a two-sided inverse glued from
1473	  the local inverses; hence the glued morphism is a global isomorphism --- the
1474	  associator. The single non-formal input of this re-route is the
1475	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the presheaf
1476	  associator (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)) and the sheaf-of-modules
1477	  internal-hom gluing are already available.
1478	
1479	  Note that, unlike the pointwise-existence statement
1480	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} --- a \emph{proposition} proved
1481	  cover-pointwise with \emph{no} overlap obligation --- the associator is
1482	  \emph{global data}, a single isomorphism of sheaves on all of \(X\). Assembling it
1483	  from local pieces is therefore genuinely stronger than that pattern: the
1484	  overlap-naturality compatibility of the local composites must be discharged before
1485	  gluing, and is discharged above. The locally-trivial hypotheses of the statement
1486	  are not consumed; the comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is applied to the
1487	  global, untrivialised restrictions \((M \otimes_X N)|_U\), so no free-cover
1488	  specialisation can replace it. The vestigial
1489	  whiskering / \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk apparatus
1490	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W,lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W,lem:jw_ismonoidal}) plays
1491	  no part in this proof and is superseded. The group law on \(\otimes\)-iso-classes
1492	  consumes only the \emph{existence} of the resulting isomorphism, never its pentagon,
1493	  triangle, or naturality coherence.
1494	\end{proof}

1496	\begin{lemma}
1497	[Left and right unitors for $\otimes_X$]
1498	  \label{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1499	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_left_unitor, AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_right_unitor}
1500	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1501	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) an
1502	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. With
1503	  \(\mathcal{O}_X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) the
1504	  structure-sheaf unit object, there are natural isomorphisms
1505	  \[
1506	    \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; M,
1507	    \qquad
1508	    M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; M .
1509	  \]
1510	\end{lemma}
1511	
1512	\begin{proof}
1513	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1514	  The presheaf monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) supplies
1515	  the left and right unitors \(\lambda, \rho\) at the presheaf level. Their
1516	  sheafifications \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}(\lambda)\),
1517	  \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}(\rho)\), composed with the sheafification
1518	  counit identifying the sheafified structure-sheaf presheaf with
1519	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (the step already used in the structure-sheaf-unit
1520	  comparison \(\mathtt{tensorObj\_unit\_iso}\) in the substrate file), give the
1521	  two isomorphisms. This route is the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern and uses no
1522	  abstract pullback.
1523	\end{proof}

1548	\begin{lemma}
1549	
1550	\leanok
1551	[Inverse of an invertible module]
1552	  \label{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
1553	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.exists_tensorObj_inverse}
1554	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
1555	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1556	  % NOTE: ON the critical path under the PRIMARY locally-trivial group law (it
1557	  % supplies inverses to lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup, in whose uses it sits).
1558	  % Only under the route-(e) fallback is it dispensable: there the group law uses
1559	  % the tensor-invertibility predicate IsInvertible (def:scheme_modules_isinvertible),
1560	  % which carries the inverse existentially, so no separate ``a tensor inverse
1561	  % exists'' lemma is required.
1562	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
1563	  % modules), Lemma~lemma-constructions-invertible (item~2: the dual of an
1564	  % invertible is invertible and the contraction map is an isomorphism) +
1565	  % Definition~definition-pic (the Picard group as the abelian group of
1566	  % iso-classes of invertibles)
1567	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4200--L4213 and L4350--L4357).
1568	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1569	  % "\begin{lemma}
1570	  % \label{lemma-constructions-invertible}
1571	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1572	  % ...
1573	  % \item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
1574	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
1575	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
1576	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X$
1577	  % is an isomorphism.
1578	  % ...
1579	  % \begin{definition}
1580	  % \label{definition-pic}
1581	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1582	  % The {\it Picard group} $\Pic(X)$ of $X$ is the
1583	  % abelian group whose elements are isomorphism classes of
1584	  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, with addition
1585	  % corresponding to tensor product.
1586	  % \end{definition}"
1587	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CR, Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-%
1588	  invertible} (item~2, the dual of an invertible is invertible and the contraction
1589	  is an isomorphism) and Definition~\texttt{definition-pic} (the Picard group is
1590	  the abelian group of iso-classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules under
1591	  tensor product).}
1592	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
1593	  bundle. The dual
1594	  \[
1595	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
1596	  \]
1597	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
1598	  isomorphism
1599	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
1600	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
1601	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
1602	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
1603	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
1604	\end{lemma}
1605	
1606	\begin{proof}
1607	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
1608	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1609	  Set \(L^{-1} := \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\).
1610	
1611	  \emph{Step 1 (\(L^{-1}\) is a line bundle).} Local triviality of \(L\)
1612	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\)) furnishes an open cover \(\{U\}\) of
1613	  \(X\) with trivialisations \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The internal hom commutes
1614	  with restriction along the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow X\), so on each \(U\)
1615	  \[
1616	    L^{-1}\big|_U \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(L|_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
1617	                 \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
1618	                 \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U ,
1619	  \]
1620	  the last identification being the evaluation-at-\(1\) isomorphism for the dual of a
1621	  free rank-one module. Hence \(L^{-1}\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an
1622	  object of \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
1623	
1624	  \emph{Step 2 (the contraction is a local isomorphism).} The evaluation/contraction
1625	  morphism
1626	  \[
1627	    \varepsilon_L \;\colon\; L \otimes_X L^{-1}
1628	      \;=\; L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
1629	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_X,
1630	    \qquad s \otimes \phi \longmapsto \phi(s),
1631	  \]
1632	  is a morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Restricting to a trivialising
1633	  open \(U\), the restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1634	  commutes \(\otimes_X\) past \((-)|_U\):
1635	  \[
1636	    (L \otimes_X L^{-1})\big|_U \;\cong\; L|_U \otimes_U L^{-1}|_U
1637	      \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U ,
1638	  \]
1639	  using the trivialisations of Step~1. Under these identifications \(\varepsilon_L|_U\)
1640	  becomes the contraction \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U \to \mathcal{O}_U\) of
1641	  the free rank-one module with its dual, which is precisely the left unitor
1642	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U (-) \cong (-)\)) and hence an
1643	  isomorphism. Thus \(\varepsilon_L|_U\) is an isomorphism on each member of the cover.
1644	
1645	  \emph{Step 3 (glue to a global isomorphism).} The local trivialisations are induced
1646	  by the open immersions with no choices, so the local contraction isomorphisms
1647	  \(\varepsilon_L|_U\) agree on the overlaps \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1648	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the unitor under further restriction. As
1649	  ``being an isomorphism'' is local on \(X\) --- a morphism of sheaves of
1650	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose restriction to every member of an open cover is an
1651	  isomorphism is itself an isomorphism --- the globally-defined \(\varepsilon_L\) is an
1652	  isomorphism \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\). Equivalently, the
1653	  local inverses glue (they agree on overlaps for the same reason) to a two-sided
1654	  inverse of \(\varepsilon_L\).
1655	
1656	  Consequently every line bundle \(L\) has the two-sided tensor inverse \(L^{-1}\) under
1657	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes of line bundles is an abelian
1658	  group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
1659	\end{proof}

1663	\leanok
1664	[Closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial carrier

1670	  Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes. The carrier is the

1672	  \[
1673	    \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T

1676	            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M \,\bigr\}
1677	  \]
1678	  introduced in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. The bifunctor
1679	  \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
1680	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to this subtype:
1681	  if \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M\) and \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M'\) then
1682	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M \otimes_{C \times_S T} M')\). Hence \(\otimes\)
1683	  closes on \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\), supplying the binary
1684	  operation the relative group law multiplies with.
1685	\end{lemma}
1686	
1687	\begin{proof}
1688	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
1689	  Closure is exactly
1690	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}: the tensor product of two
1691	  locally-trivial rank-one modules is again locally trivial of rank one
1692	  (\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_isLocallyTrivial}\)). The Lean construction
1693	  \(\mathtt{tensorObjOnProduct}\) applies this directly to a pair of objects of
1694	  the \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) subtype \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\),
1695	  returning the tensor with its locally-trivial witness; no
1696	  \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate and no coherence isomorphism enters this
1697	  closure statement. (The \(\otimes\)-invertibility carrier
1698	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible} is a
1699	  separate predicate, connected to \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) only by an
1700	  off-critical-path equivalence; the units-group packaging is
1701	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, not this lemma.)
1702	\end{proof}

1704	\begin{lemma}
1705	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
1706	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
1707	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.LineBundle.OnProduct.pullback_tensorObj_iso}
1708	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1709	  % NOTE: pullback preserves the structure sheaf and tensor products (and hence
1710	  % tensor-invertibility) by the standard pullback-tensor compatibility of
1711	  % \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules (Stacks \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} /
1712	  % \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}), independently of the open-immersion
1713	  % restriction-compatibility iso.
1714	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
1715	  functor
1716	  \[
1717	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
1718	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
1719	  \]
1720	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
1721	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
1722	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
1723	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
1724	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
1725	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
1726	  canonical isomorphism
1727	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
1728	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
1729	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
1730	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
1731	\end{lemma}
1732	
1733	\begin{proof}
1734	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1735	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
1736	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
1737	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
1738	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
1739	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
1740	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
1741	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\): on each
1742	  affine open where \(\mathcal{N}\) and \(\mathcal{N}'\) are free of rank one
1743	  over \(A\), both sides evaluate to the free rank-one \(B\)-module, so
1744	  the comparison is an isomorphism. No flatness of \(A \to B\) is required
1745	  at this level, as the modules involved are locally free. The compatibility
1746	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
1747	  same cover.
1748	\end{proof}

1750	\section{\(\otimes\)-invertibility and the commutative-monoid group-law engine}
1751	\label{sec:tensorobj_invertibility}
1752	
1753	The relative Picard group law is assembled from \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertibility}
1754	and the monoidal coherence isomorphisms of
1755	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
1756	following Mathlib's Picard-group idiom (\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\),
1757	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic} = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\),
1758	\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\); \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) as the
1759	design template. Under the route~(e) fallback the inverse of an invertible object
1760	is carried by the predicate itself, so neither the open-immersion
1761	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} nor a
1762	separate tensor-inverse lemma is needed there. Under the PRIMARY locally-trivial
1763	route, by contrast, both \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (via the PRIMARY proof
1764	of \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}) and the tensor-inverse lemma
1765	\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (supplying inverses to
1766	\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) are on the critical path.

1889	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
1890	  objects of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
1891	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) form a commutative monoid under the
1892	  substrate tensor product \(\otimes_X\) in which every element is a unit ---
1893	  equivalently, an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag
1894	  01CR).
1895	
1896	  \textbf{Primary construction (directly on locally-trivial iso-classes, à la
1897	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\)).} The commutative group is built directly on
1898	  isomorphism classes of \emph{locally-trivial} (equivalently \(\otimes\)-invertible)
1899	  sheaves, mirroring Mathlib's ring-level Picard group
1900	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
1901	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The group operation is
1902	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\), well-defined on iso-classes by the
1903	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
1904	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) and again locally trivial by
1905	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}; the identity is
1906	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and the inverse of \([L]\) is the class of the dual
1907	  \([L^{-1}]\) supplied by \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (with
1908	  \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)).
1909	
1910	  The four group-axiom fields are defined \emph{by hand}, each discharged by a
1911	  \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism read as an equality of iso-classes --- passage
1912	  to iso-classes turns an isomorphism of sheaves into an equality of classes:
1913	  \[
1914	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \lambda_X \rangle,
1915	    \quad
1916	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \rho_X \rangle,
1917	    \quad
1918	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \alpha_{X,Y,Z} \rangle,
1919	    \quad
1920	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \beta_{X,Y} \rangle,
1921	  \]
1922	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity is
1923	  the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
1924	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, each consumed only as the proposition
1925	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\); the pentagon, triangle and hexagon
1926	  coherence carried by a \(\MonoidalCategory\) typeclass is \emph{never} invoked in
1927	  any of the four axiom proofs.
1928	
1929	  This by-hand assembly does \emph{not} go through
1930	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)
1931	  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}): those constructions
1932	  \emph{require} a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\) (and, for the commutative
1933	  law, \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on the carrier category --- precisely the
1934	  coherent monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) over the \emph{varying}
1935	  structure sheaf that this construction explicitly avoids. Consequently the group law
1936	  requires \emph{no} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \emph{no}
1937	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\) instance on
1938	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The genuine precedent is Mathlib's fixed-ring
1939	  Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
1940	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}):
1941	  there the \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) \emph{is} read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the
1942	  coherent monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\)
1943	  that Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring. The present construction \emph{cannot} take
1944	  that \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route --- no coherent monoidal category on
1945	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is available --- so
1946	  its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is built directly from the existence-of-isomorphism
1947	  propositions above, the construction ultimately resting, as
1948	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) does, on iso-classes of invertible modules. The pinned
1949	  carrier \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}\) realizes this commutative-group
1950	  structure; the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at the
1951	  pinned commit.
1952	
1953	  This is the \emph{lower-risk} path: every ingredient is either already proved
1954	  (the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, the braiding
1955	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}) or locally-trivial-scoped and assembled (the
1956	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}). The only remaining obligations on this
1957	  critical path are the restriction-compatibility comparison
1958	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (which the primary whisker route of
1959	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} also now consumes) and the
1960	  dual/inverse \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. In particular the group law
1961	  never invokes the stalk--tensor commutation (d.2) over a varying ring.
1962	
1963	  \textbf{Fallback (full route~(e)).} Alternatively, once
1964	  \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} lands, \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X =
1965	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is a full (symmetric)
1966	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) via \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\),
1967	  and the Picard group is the units of its skeleton,
1968	  \(\Pic(X) = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\),
1969	  exactly as Mathlib builds \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R =
1970	  \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\) and transports
1971	  \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\). This fallback yields the group on \emph{all}
1972	  invertible modules and is retained for the full monoidal generality, but it is
1973	  not required for the relative Picard group law, which the primary construction
1974	  already supplies.
1975	\end{lemma}

2033	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
2034	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
2035	
2036	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
2037	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean

2040	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
2041	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.

2046	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
2047	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
2048	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
2049	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2050	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
2051	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2052	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
2053	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
2054	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
2055	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
2056	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
2057	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
2058	
2059	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
2060	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
2061	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
2062	  % groups)
2063	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
2064	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2065	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
2066	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
2067	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
2068	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
2069	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
2070	  % \end{dfn}
2071	  %  [\dots]
2072	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
2073	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
2074	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
2075	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
2076	  % topologies  by
2077	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
2078	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
2079	  %  \end{dfn}"
2080	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
2081	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
2082	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
2083	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
2084	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
2085	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
2086	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
2087	  \[
2088	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
2089	  \]
2090	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
2091	  abelian-group structure with addition
2092	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
2093	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
2094	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
2095	  inverse
2096	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
2097	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
2098	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2099	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
2100	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
2101	  map
2102	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
2103	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
2104	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2105	\end{theorem}
2106	
2107	\begin{proof}
2108	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2109	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
2110	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2111	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
2112	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
2113	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
2114	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
2115	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
2116	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
2117	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
2118	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
2119	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
2120	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
2121	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
2122	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
2123	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
2124	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
2125	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
2126	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
2127	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
2128	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
2129	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
2130	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
2131	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
2132	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
2133	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
2134	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
2135	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
2136	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
2137	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
2138	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
2139	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
2140	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
2141	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
2142	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
2143	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
2144	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
2145	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
2146	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
2147	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
2148	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
2149	
2150	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
2151	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
2152	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
2153	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
2154	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
2155	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
2156	
2157	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
2158	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
2159	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
2160	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
2161	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
2162	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
2163	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
2164	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
2165	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
2166	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2167	\end{proof}
